text: when you're looking at productivity tools like Cursor and others that a lot of our engineers are using, because I would say it's like, what's the measurement you do? It's not necessarily lines of code written, because that could also create somewhat perverse incentives. So you have really long code base versus a more efficient one. So what we're looking at is the rate of pull requests. So how frequently are we seeing our engineers ship code as a proxy metric to understanding overall efficiency for our engineering team. But I think the proof will be in the pudding of like, do we actually find that we're increasing the product velocity overall? And that's something that we'll probably have to have more of a sense for over a six or 12 month horizon. You're probably not going to get as much signal in the first month or so. And so just being, I would say, upfront with folk when they're looking at using tools or especially when they're looking at making a meaningful investment in a different AI platform or tool is, okay, what do you think success looks like? Like when you actually even just ask the question of like, how will you define success of using this tool? What is the outcome you want to see? At least gets the buyer in the mindset of like, OK, there is an outcome that we're expecting. And then we can see if, you know, if it's a proxy metric or more directional sense in terms of Sierra, like, do we actually achieve that metric? And it's not necessarily to say, like, if we miss it, like, you know, that person is going to get like wrapped on the wrist. But it's more, OK, what was wrong in our assumptions going into that? And how can we learn about that for future sort of deployments of different tooling? I think it should be pretty clear what is driving value or not. I'm just thinking of sort of a couple of examples on our end. When I first joined myself, we had like one lawyer and had a massive external legal counsel bill. And then we started using GCAI, which is not free, but it's not super expensive either. And suddenly our external counsel bill like dropped because we no longer needed to send a bunch of extra work to external counsel because the easy stuff GCAI can do is internal counsel can spend more of their time on value-added work. And the other one is we built an agent to review all our inbound leads. If you hit contact sales and you fill out the form, we used to have a 10-person team that would review that list. It would delete people that would just scream obscenities at us. Or sometimes for people using it to contact support instead. I have a question on this thing. My X, Y, Z isn't working. And then once they sort of drill that list down into actual leads then they would spend at the same time kind of what we call hydrating the lead of like is this like a real business What does their website infrastructure look like Like what are their needs What geo are they in And now all that stuff is automated So we went from having 10 people for U.S. inbound leads to one person. And the only thing this person does is to make sure that the deal bot is working. So this is like pretty clear. Like obviously this is working. And we have a bunch of other things that are like probably not working quite as well. But when AI is working, you don't need to do some ROI analysis to assess is this working or not. When you started thinking about Sierra, or GC.ai, did you ask them to give you a estimate or some ROI calculator of sorts? So we went through an initial backtest with them. So we basically said, like, we essentially had an upfront almost test of, okay, if we were to run all these chats through Sierra, what was its efficacy in terms of getting to a resolution? And that was one of the reasons we went with Sierra over some of the other platforms that we were doing similar tests with, was just to understand what was our confidence interval that they would actually be able to resolve the case that was being chatted in. we then actually did a pilot period for about three months that we essentially wanted to see okay like how do we sort of expose let's call it five percent of chats to sierra and then ten percent and then as we were both getting confidence about that efficacy of it and that it wasn't going to cause a worse customer experience and also that when it helped us better quantify okay this is the savings it could otherwise drive and therefore the knowledge of the roi up front was actually pretty easy for us to determine, even before sort of signing up to a more sort of established agreement that is now over 100% of our chat cases, for example. So you believe that is that the model for bringing AI into the enterprise, that you have to go through this try before buy? Yeah, that's a good question. I don't think you necessarily have to go through that. I think it's easier, for sure, if you have the ability to. What we're seeing with a lot of our enterprise customers is that pilots, I would say in general, and this is like one man's opinion, so I take it with a grain of salt, I think has like almost a 50-50 success rate. And what I mean by that is a pilot can either be really good if you have like a very defined, this is what the pilot's for, this is how we're going to be evaluated, this is what success looks like. And you align with your counterparty whether it your customer or your vendor up front about what those look like and then you work across the pilot towards that and you get to the end with real signal to make a decision either to buy or not I think where pilots go horribly wrong is when you very vague up front about what does success look like, and you kind of just run a three-month experimentation with a vendor or with a customer, and then you get to the end and you're like, well, wait, how do we even evaluate if we're happy with what the prior three months has achieved? And so from that perspective, I think that pilots can be a useful tool, whether it's to gain customers through saying, hey, we'll do a pilot with you, or as a buyer to say, like, yes, we'll sign up for a pilot. I think it's just really important in either respect that you have a clear definition of what success looks like and make it quite targeted so that at the end of that period, you actually can make a go-no-go decision. So actually just thinking through this, so all of our sort of big customers for VZero, which is our air product today, I think all of them started as like a 10-seat account. And then it became like a 20-seat account, and then 50, and then 100, and then 800. And the few times you've done like, oh, they want to do like 1,000 trial seats for three months, and come back after three months and be like, well, it turns out that only like 60 people ended up using it. So like we don't think this is for us, and like it didn't work out. We've had much better success for this like gradual, you get in there with like a group of people that actually want to use it, even if it is smaller. And then they hear, hey, do you hear what the team over marketing are doing? They did this prototyping thing. We should check that out. And then you get a few people from the design team, and then someone else hears about it. You kind of get that organic growth, as opposed to this from the top, push down, hey, you now have all seed access to this thing, and they sort of feel like the tools are being forced upon them, which has not really worked out at all for us, versus the bottoms up. It takes a little longer NDR looks really good though, so like the finance person in you should like it. Tushar, Maxwell you have your go-to-market strategy, calibrate it now? We get ready, we got all three of you there. Land and expand. The buyers. And just kind of a time check, I know we're coming out, do we want to end the round? We've got to end it. Okay. Great. Thinking about Sarah, by the way, and I'm a finance leader, thinking about driving efficiency within FP&A, some of the things that take us the longest time and add the most value is driving and identifying insights across the business Also as strategies change throughout the year how do we pivot the business more the downstream impacts Have you used anything in your companies to kind of identify those insights and or understand the downstream impacts to a strategic change across the company That's probably the, I talked earlier about complex, non-deterministic workflows. That is like exactly it. The closest we've done is the ability to make it easier for people to sort of prompt our Snowflake database so that you can at least get access to information. You can experiment a little bit more with what if I cut the data this way? What about this thing? But it's still and I think of this as like the best part of like FPNS, stratfin is when they think sort of strategically about the business and insights. So that's also probably one of the last things to be able to be truly replaced or even enhanced meaningfully. by AI. Yeah, we can make it a little bit faster today. And I actually just saw a demo of something called Concourse, which, yeah, it's basically this middleware that connects all your different data sources, and they have an LLM on top of it that allows you to search, say, like, Snowflake, Stripe, and a few other things, NetSuite. But it's still pretty early days for all these different things. But yeah, there's hope for the future. I would say I really like to kind of geek out on kind of like the Gartner and Forrester type research. And at the Gartner CFO conference earlier this year, they did studies about that you can't really solve kind of the FP&A burnout issue through just like being more efficient and kind of getting automation. you need to kind of create these decision support tools like for folks so that they can use those tools. And then you don't like the FP&A business partner doesn't have to kind of be present for them when that decision is being made. So it's like, how can you kind of leverage the technology and kind of multiply yourself to make yourself available to the entire organization? And I think the, like for us internally at Seek Geek, we're a live entertainment ticketing company. And when we're, we've had a lot of success with kind of rhythm of the business type use cases within AI where, hey, that variance report, like from Looker, from Tableau that would say like, here is like our market share data, here's what the year over years look like, but kind of go out into the news and say, oh, play your injury reports, like all these other factors to help kind of free up our resources,
